questions.question: What is the single most important factor in whether technology helps or harms your mental health?

options.text: The total amount of screen time per day
Whether you use it intentionally (with purpose) or out of habit (passively)
What specific apps or platforms you're using
Whether you use it on mobile devices or desktop computers

questions.explanation: The 2026 SEM study (n=842) found that intentional, goal-directed use predicted better wellbeing (β=0.37) and attentional control (β=0.42) — independent of total screen time. Passive, habitual use showed negative associations with both.

questions.question: What defines 'intentional technology use'?

options.text: Using technology only for work, never for entertainment
Using technology with a clear goal, aligned with your values, with self-regulation and awareness
Never using technology unless absolutely necessary
Only using technology during designated time blocks

questions.explanation: Intentional use has four components: (1) clear goal/purpose, (2) values alignment, (3) self-regulation (choosing when to start and stop), and (4) awareness of how it makes you feel. It's not about avoiding technology — it's about using it purposefully.

questions.question: Which scenario best represents passive/habitual technology use?

options.text: Setting a 20-minute timer to watch YouTube tutorials for learning
Opening Instagram to check on a friend recovering from surgery
Automatically opening TikTok while waiting for the bus without thinking
Scheduling a video call with family members

questions.explanation: Passive use = no clear goal, automatic behavior, scrolling until external event ends it (bus arrives). The other options show intentional use with clear goals and self-regulation.

questions.question: What does the research say about the simple 'passive vs. active' social media distinction?

options.text: It's perfectly clear — passive scrolling is always worse than active posting
The distinction is oversimplified and context-dependent (MODERATE-CONTESTED evidence)
Active posting always produces better mental health outcomes
The distinction has been disproven entirely

questions.explanation: A meta-analysis of 141 studies (Godard & Holtzman, 2024) found that the simple passive/active dichotomy is oversimplified and context-dependent. It's more nuanced than 'passive bad, active good.'

questions.question: Choose one technology habit you have (checking social media, news apps, video streaming, etc.). Is your use mostly intentional or mostly passive? How could you make it more intentional?
